skill.name: building-native-ui
skill.description: Guidance for building native-feeling Expo Router apps with routes, tabs, iOS controls, animations, media, storage, and 3D UI patterns.
skill.summary: Build polished Expo Router interfaces with native navigation, controls, media, storage, animation, and visual effect patterns.
skill.author: expo

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, storage, filesystem, and prompt-injection indicators, but review found they are Markdown documentation examples for Expo and React Native development. No prompt injection text, credential exfiltration, executable installer, or malicious automation was found; publication is acceptable with low risk because the skill teaches app code that may request permissions or use local storage.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are Markdown fences, inline code, and Expo command examples. They are not executable skill code and do not create command injection by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled locations show Markdown and TypeScript documentation context, not Ruby backtick execution. The repeated detections align with code fences and inline examples across the reference files.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The network indicators point to an Apple documentation link and example audio or video sources. I found no evidence that the skill sends user data, credentials, or telemetry to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in user-facing documentation or sample playback code. No surrounding code indicates exfiltration, credential handling, or background network activity.

low_findings.title: Storage Examples Are App Guidance, Not Credential Access
low_findings.description: The storage reference teaches localStorage and SQLite examples and explicitly directs sensitive data such as tokens and passwords to expo-secure-store. This is not evidence of marketplace credential theft.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are framed as app data storage patterns, and the sensitive-data row recommends secure storage. The risk is limited to developers applying the examples incorrectly.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Is React Lazy Loading Guidance
low_findings.description: The dynamic import finding is a React.lazy example for loading a local scene component. It does not import from user-controlled input or a remote URL.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The import target is a static local alias and the surrounding section describes code splitting. There is no dynamic expression built from untrusted data.

low_findings.title: Environment And Path Findings Are Benign Framework Examples
low_findings.description: The environment access is a recommendation to use Expo platform metadata, and the path traversal alert is a normal relative import. Neither indicates secret access or filesystem traversal.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable name is EXPO_OS, not a secret, and the relative import targets an application component. No file reads, writes, or traversal over user input were found.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found In Reviewed Matches
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged a system-prompt pattern, but the reviewed line contains PlatformColor("systemBackground") inside a React Native view style. No override, skip-audit, or system-message instruction was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious token is part of an iOS color constant in UI code. A broader phrase search found no explicit prompt injection language in SKILL.md or the reference files.

content.value_statement: Expo apps often need native-feeling navigation, controls, media, and visual polish. This skill gives Codex and Claude practical patterns for Expo Router UI work.
actual_capabilities: Guides Expo Router route structure, stack layouts, modal routes, shared groups, and dynamic routes.
Provides native tab patterns using expo-router unstable native tabs and SF Symbols.
Shows iOS-style controls, form sheets, blur, glass effects, gradients, and responsive safe-area handling.
Includes Reanimated patterns for entering, exiting, layout, scroll, and gesture-driven animations.
Covers media capture, playback, file saving, local storage, SQLite, SecureStore, WebGPU, and Three.js examples.
limitations: It is guidance content, not a runnable Expo project or component library.
Some patterns depend on current Expo SDK support, platform availability, or iOS-specific APIs.
Examples may need adaptation for Android, web, accessibility, permissions, and app-specific data models.
It does not replace human review for privacy, app store compliance, or production security choices.

use_cases.title: Create an Expo Router app shell
use_cases.description: Use the route, stack, tab, modal, and shared group guidance to structure a mobile app from the first screen.

use_cases.title: Upgrade a React Native UI to native patterns
use_cases.description: Replace generic controls with Expo-friendly navigation, native tabs, SF Symbols, blur effects, and form sheets.

use_cases.title: Prototype media and visual experiences
use_cases.description: Use camera, audio, video, animation, gradient, WebGPU, and Three.js references to explore richer app interactions.

prompt_templates.title: Start a native Expo screen
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one polished screen.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the building-native-ui skill to create an Expo Router screen for a settings page. Follow the native controls, safe-area, and styling guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Design tabs and stacks
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the building-native-ui skill to design an Expo Router layout with native tabs, a search tab, and stack screens for detail pages.

prompt_templates.title: Add motion and media
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the building-native-ui skill to add Reanimated transitions, camera capture, and media saving to an Expo app while keeping permissions clear.

prompt_templates.title: Audit and refactor an app UI
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the building-native-ui skill to review my Expo Router app structure and propose changes for routes, tabs, storage, visual effects, and platform-specific APIs.

output_examples.input: Create a native-feeling event app layout with tabs and search.
output: A route plan with native tabs for events and search.
Stack screen recommendations for event details and modal forms.
UI guidance for safe areas, selectable text, SF Symbols, and search behavior.

output_examples.input: Improve a camera screen in an Expo app.
output: A permission-aware camera flow with clear empty states.
Native-style controls for capture, gallery, flash, and camera switching.
Recommendations for haptics, safe-area spacing, and media library permission timing.

output_examples.input: Add a lightweight local data layer for preferences and events.
output: A storage choice matrix for preferences, complex queries, and sensitive values.
A reactive preference pattern for app settings.
SQLite guidance for structured event data and parameterized queries.

best_practices: Start with Expo Go, then use custom builds only when native modules or platform targets require them.
Keep route files in the app directory and move shared components, utilities, and types outside it.
Use native Expo libraries and platform conventions before adding generic web or legacy React Native APIs.
anti_patterns: Do not co-locate components, types, or utilities inside the Expo Router app directory.
Do not use removed or legacy modules such as React Native AsyncStorage, WebView, Picker, or expo-av.
Do not store tokens or passwords in localStorage or SQLite when SecureStore is the intended option.

faq.question: Does this skill generate a complete Expo app?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples that Codex, Claude, or Claude Code can apply inside your project.

faq.question: Can it help with Expo Router navigation?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers route files, stacks, native tabs, shared groups, modals, search routes, and dynamic segments.

faq.question: Does it support iOS-specific interface patterns?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for SF Symbols, native tabs, form sheets, blur effects, glass effects, and Apple-style controls.

faq.question: Can I use it for media features?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for camera access, image picking, audio playback, audio recording, video playback, and saving media.

faq.question: Does it address data storage?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains localStorage, SQLite, and SecureStore choices for preferences, larger data sets, and sensitive values.

faq.question: What should I review before production use?
faq.answer: Review permissions, accessibility, platform support, secure storage, app store rules, and dependency compatibility for your Expo SDK.
